question: Why does Jacobson's RTO formula include a 4 * RTTVAR term rather than just SRTT?
options: A. Because RFC 6298 requires it for compatibility with BSD
B. Because the variance term prevents spurious retransmits when queueing delay spikes under load
C. Because TCP must always send data at line rate
D. Because the 4x factor matches the standard deviation for a Gaussian RTT distribution
explanation: On real networks, RTT is not constant -- it spikes when queues build up under load. A fixed 2xRTT timeout (the original 1981 rule) fires too early during queueing spikes, producing spurious retransmits that worsen congestion. Jacobson's insight was that the RTO must also track variance: 4 x RTTVAR (chosen so that ~99% of samples fall within RTO under an approximately Gaussian model).

question: Why does Karn's algorithm forbid RTT sampling from retransmitted segments?
options: A. Because retransmitted segments carry a different IP identification field
B. Because the ack might be for the original or the retransmission, and the kernel cannot tell which -- using either would corrupt SRTT/RTTVAR
C. Because the checksum is different on retransmitted segments
D. Because retransmitted segments always have RTT less than 1 ms
explanation: When the retransmission timer fires, the sender sends a second copy of the segment. When an ack arrives, the kernel cannot tell whether the ack acknowledges the first copy or the second -- the ACK number alone is the same in both cases. Sampling that RTT into SRTT/RTTVAR would mix the original RTT (good) with the retransmission RTT (which already includes the timeout, very bad). Karn's fix: skip RTT sampling for any retransmitted segment, and double the RTO on each timeout until a fresh (non-retransmitted) sample arrives.

question: Why does TCP Reno prefer fast retransmit (after 3 duplicate acks) over waiting for the retransmission timer?
options: A. Because duplicate acks are encrypted and timers are not
B. Because the timer is at least 1 second long and that delay hurts throughput; three duplicate acks strongly imply single-packet loss, so the sender can retransmit immediately
C. Because the receiver demands it
D. Because fast retransmit uses less CPU than timers
explanation: A retransmission timer is at least 1 second (RFC 6298 minimum RTO). If the sender can detect loss from three duplicate acks -- which arrives within a few milliseconds of the original transmission -- it can retransmit without waiting for the timer. This is the difference between TCP Tahoe (drops to slow start on any loss) and TCP Reno (fast retransmit + fast recovery). Reno's saw-tooth cwnd pattern achieves much higher throughput on links with occasional single-packet loss.
